caveats: a: The occupation mix is for NAICS 513100, not newspaper publishers alone.
a: Published occupation shares are employment-based; the wage-weighted task share is a modeled bridge rather than a direct measurement.
a: AI assistance with a draft or analysis is not equivalent to substituting original reporting, verification, source trust, or final editorial accountability.
a: The frozen labor-share input is an ancestor-sector estimate and may not represent newspaper economics.
rho: The executive survey is global, not limited to US LMM newspaper publishers.
rho: Perceived newsroom transformation is not implemented substitution of exposed labor.
rho: Audience acceptance is higher for assistive than mostly automated journalism, requiring continued human review.
e: No direct audit of LMM newspaper firms against the recurring outsourced-service lens was found.
e: Publishing an owned information product is not the same as providing an outsourced service, even when revenue recurs.
e: The frozen dataset declares no defensible n-band value, so the eligible-firm count will remain missing.
e: Local titles range from transferable multi-title operators to owner-dependent microbusinesses.
s5: The source counts newspaper titles and transactions, not eligible LMM legal entities.
s5: A multi-title chain transaction can move many papers in one control event.
s5: The active 2024 market may not persist for five years, and some distressed titles close rather than transfer.
q: No direct evidence measures five-year post-AI benefit retention for LMM newspapers.
q: Advertising and reader revenue respond differently to cost reductions and quality changes.
q: Declining demand can force savings into survival or product reinvestment rather than durable cash retention.
d5: Employment, output, circulation, and revenue are different quantities from constant-quality service demand.
d5: The long-run output trend extends through 2022 and may not represent the next five years.
d5: National digital subscription growth is concentrated in a few large papers and may not transfer to LMM titles.
d5: The range includes contraction but not a complete collapse of socially valuable reporting demand.

o: Social and AI distribution can preserve demand for information while removing traffic and revenue from the publisher.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Readers pay recurring print or digital subscriptions and single-copy charges; advertisers and sponsors buy recurring access to an audience; additional revenue can come from events, content licensing, memberships, and commercial services. The publisher owns or controls the editorial product and distributes it directly or through platforms.
lens.excluded_activities: Shells, captive internal publications, printing-only operations, non-control financing vehicles, inactive titles, individual blogs or creator channels outside NAICS 513110, and outlets whose reporting relationships, brand, systems, or revenue depend on an owner and cannot continue after a control transfer.
lens.included_activities: United States lower-middle-market newspaper publishers with transferable local, regional, business, or specialist titles and recurring relationships with subscribers, advertisers, sponsors, licensors, or institutional customers across print and digital formats.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations and wages for the broader NAICS 513100 publishing group rather than six-digit newspaper publishers. That group has large editorial, media, sales, business, and administrative populations; BLS task descriptions show that research, drafting, rewriting, proofreading, fact-check support, layout, audience analysis, ad preparation, records, and routine multi-format adaptation are text- and data-intensive. Those tasks are mapped to the share of current newspaper labor that could be substituted or avoid hiring, while interviews, source development, original field reporting, final editorial judgment, local sales relationships, and accountable publication remain human-led. The broader group includes periodicals, books, and directories, which may overstate exposure if their production is more standardized, while newspaper deadline repetition may understate it.
caveats: The occupation mix is for NAICS 513100, not newspaper publishers alone.
Published occupation shares are employment-based; the wage-weighted task share is a modeled bridge rather than a direct measurement.
AI assistance with a draft or analysis is not equivalent to substituting original reporting, verification, source trust, or final editorial accountability.
The frozen labor-share input is an ancestor-sector estimate and may not represent newspaper economics.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted time study of newspaper staff, separating already automated from remaining tasks in reporting, editing, design, advertising, circulation, customer service, administration, printing, and distribution, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: News gathering and editorial work contain substantial searchable research, transcription, summarization, first-draft, copy-editing, headline, tagging, archive, personalization, and format-conversion work. Advertising operations, subscription service, finance, scheduling, and routine analytics add further exposure. Relationship reporting, investigations, physical newsgathering, verification, public accountability, sensitive editing, and consultative local selling constrain substitution.

d5.bridge: BLS projects newspaper-publisher employment to fall 20.0 percent from 2024 to 2034, while its long-run 1987-2022 series reports newspaper output falling 4.2 percent annually. Pew reports long-running circulation and advertising decline alongside digital-subscription gains concentrated in major titles. These employment, output, circulation, and revenue indicators are translated into the year-five constant-price demand ratio for the current newspaper service basket. Employment can fall faster than demand as productivity rises, and historical print decline may overstate future decline for successful digital subscriptions; platform substitution and local closures can instead make it understate downside.
caveats: Employment, output, circulation, and revenue are different quantities from constant-quality service demand.
The long-run output trend extends through 2022 and may not represent the next five years.
National digital subscription growth is concentrated in a few large papers and may not transfer to LMM titles.
The range includes contraction but not a complete collapse of socially valuable reporting demand.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast for active 513110 publishers combining real subscription and advertising revenue, paid digital accounts, churn, audience engagement, print frequency, closures, starts, platform referrals, and content licensing would materially change the multiplier.
d5.rationale: Demand for verified local, business, civic, investigative, and specialist reporting persists, and paid digital products can offset some print losses. The current basket nevertheless faces continuing print attrition, weak advertising, platform disintermediation, shrinking search and social referral traffic, consolidation, and substitution by free digital sources.

caveats: No direct audit of LMM newspaper firms against the recurring outsourced-service lens was found.
Publishing an owned information product is not the same as providing an outsourced service, even when revenue recurs.
The frozen dataset declares no defensible n-band value, so the eligible-firm count will remain missing.
Local titles range from transferable multi-title operators to owner-dependent microbusinesses.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit firm register joining publisher ownership, title activity, subscription and advertising mix, recurring institutional contracts, normalized earnings, owner dependence, audience concentration, and transferability would materially change the share.
e.rationale: The official definition describes publishers creating and distributing an owned editorial product and selling advertising, so most firms are not conventional outsourced-service providers. A minority fit through recurring, externally paid advertising, sponsorship, licensing, custom-content, institutional-information, or membership relationships and possess transferable titles, archives, audience data, staff, and systems. Eligibility is reduced by distressed or inactive titles, owner-editor dependence, captive community or political purpose, weak normalized earnings, platform dependence, and the absence of a defensible LMM firm denominator.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 51 ($506,769,522,547) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 51 ($2,139,449,301,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 51. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3593 (margins.json[513]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Software (System & Application) EBITDA/Sales 35.93% (NAICS 2022 513 = publishing incl. software)); revenue/employee $588,729 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 51); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: No direct substitution study estimates the operator-required share for NAICS 513110.
National and specialist titles differ sharply from local community newspapers.
Social and AI distribution can preserve demand for information while removing traffic and revenue from the publisher.
o.change_evidence: Market-by-market studies comparing publisher-produced and platform- or AI-supplied news on original reporting share, factual accuracy, corrections, local coverage, advertiser outcomes, willingness to pay, and audience substitution would materially change the share.
o.rationale: An accountable publisher remains necessary for source cultivation, interviews, investigations, verification, corrections, legal review, community presence, editorial standards, advertiser stewardship, rights, and brand trust. Aggregators, social platforms, newsletters, creators, public records, wire copy, and AI summaries can eliminate or self-serve commodity updates and routine repackaging, so a meaningful share of the current basket may no longer require a newspaper operator.

caveats: No direct evidence measures five-year post-AI benefit retention for LMM newspapers.
Advertising and reader revenue respond differently to cost reductions and quality changes.
Declining demand can force savings into survival or product reinvestment rather than durable cash retention.
q.change_evidence: Publisher-level panels linking AI deployment and labor hours to subscription pricing, churn, advertising yields, platform shares, content volume, software and review costs, and normalized operating cash flow over renewals would materially change the retained share.
q.rationale: Subscription prices, fixed advertising packages, sponsorships, and licensing generally do not reset automatically when production cost falls, allowing implemented benefits to be retained initially. Retention erodes through renewal discounts, advertiser performance demands, competitive subscription pricing, platform revenue sharing, higher output expectations, severance and transition costs, and the need to reinvest in differentiated reporting, product, security, and audience growth.

rho.bridge: The Reuters Institute surveyed 326 news executives in 51 countries and found 63 percent saying newsrooms were being transformed somewhat and 24 percent fully by generative AI. This measures executive perception across international news organizations, not the fraction of exposed work implemented in US LMM newspapers. It is translated downward into five-year workflow coverage because small publishers face weak data infrastructure, limited training, integration costs, union and policy constraints, hallucination and defamation risk, audience skepticism, and the need for human review. Executive selection may overstate adoption, while rapid integration into commodity publishing tools may make the estimate conservative.
caveats: The executive survey is global, not limited to US LMM newspaper publishers.
Perceived newsroom transformation is not implemented substitution of exposed labor.
Audience acceptance is higher for assistive than mostly automated journalism, requiring continued human review.
rho.change_evidence: A representative US newspaper survey linking deployed AI tools to workflow coverage, staff hours, avoided hires, correction rates, legal incidents, audience trust, union rules, and software cost by publisher size would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Research assistance, transcription, first drafts, summaries, headlines, metadata, archive retrieval, customer service, ad proposals, and routine analytics can be introduced through existing newsroom, CRM, and publishing systems. Implementation will be slower for investigative and local reporting, sensitive stories, image verification, final copy approval, and workflows where an error damages trust or creates legal exposure.

s5.bridge: Medill tracked 268 newspaper titles changing ownership in 77 transactions during its 2024 update, against slightly fewer than 5,600 remaining newspapers. That title-level one-year flow is translated into a five-year probability that an eligible LMM publisher undergoes a qualifying control transfer. Multi-title transactions are deduplicated conceptually, closures and non-control arrangements are excluded, and the range allows for consolidation pressure and owner succession. Title counts can overstate firm-level transfer frequency because one deal may contain many papers; distressed outlets can also be untransferable, while the report may miss private single-title deals.
caveats: The source counts newspaper titles and transactions, not eligible LMM legal entities.
A multi-title chain transaction can move many papers in one control event.
The active 2024 market may not persist for five years, and some distressed titles close rather than transfer.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census matching 513110 legal entities to titles, enterprise value, normalized earnings, deal structure, control conveyed, owner age, closures, failed sales, and post-close continuity would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Newspaper ownership is actively changing, including small local purchases and chain acquisitions. Financial pressure, family succession, and consolidation create transfer events, but closures, nonprofit conversions, bundled title sales, lender control, distressed assets, and owner-dependent operations do not always produce a qualifying transferable LMM firm.

principal_driver: Research, drafting, editing, audience, advertising, and administrative workflows create a broad AI-assistable task base.
principal_weakness: The industry combines a weak outsourced-service fit with declining demand and no defensible LMM firm denominator.
